A cost-aware semantic gating system for scalable LLM reliability in Skinnova
When building Skinnova for the AI Partner Catalyst: Accelerate Innovation Hackathon , we encountered a fundamental systems problem:
Large Language Models do not fail structurally most of the time — they fail semantically while still producing valid outputs.
This creates a difficult engineering constraint:

• outputs are syntactically valid

• but potentially factually incorrect or unsafe

• and expensive to verify at scale using Judge LLMs
To address this, we introduced Selective Hallucination Evaluation (SHE) — a pre-evaluation semantic risk gating mechanism designed to decide whether a response should be escalated to a Judge LLM.

1. Motivation: Why SHE is needed
In conventional LLM pipelines, hallucination evaluation is typically implemented as:
LLM → Judge LLM → Final OutputThis approach has three major limitations:

• Linear cost scaling with traffic

• Redundant evaluation of low-risk responses

• High latency overhead due to synchronous Judge LLM calls
More importantly, it treats all outputs as equally likely to contain harmful hallucinations, which is empirically incorrect in production workloads.
In Skinnova, we observed that:

• a large fraction of responses are low-risk (e.g., ingredient explanations)

• only a small subset requires semantic verification (e.g., safety claims, routines)
This motivated a selective evaluation strategy.

2. Scope definition: SHE does not handle structural failures
A critical design constraint is that SHE operates only on semantically valid outputs.
SHE explicitly does NOT address:

• invalid JSON

• missing fields

• schema violations

• truncated outputs

• parsing errors
These are handled by deterministic system components such as:

• schema validators

• contract enforcement layers

• response parsers
Formally, if ( X ) is the full output space and ( S ⊂ X ) is the set of syntactically valid outputs:
Thus, SHE is strictly a semantic risk evaluator, not a structural validator.

3. Core idea: risk-gated evaluation instead of uniform judgment
The central principle of SHE is:
Not all outputs require expensive semantic verification.
Instead of sending every response to a Judge LLM, we define a gating function:
where:
Only responses exceeding this threshold are passed to the Judge LLM.

4. Risk model formulation
SHE defines a linear additive risk model over semantic categories.
Let:
Each category corresponds to a predefined hallucination-prone semantic region (e.g., medical claims, absolute guarantees).
Each category has:
Risk function:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This formulation enables:

• deterministic evaluation

• interpretability of risk contributions

• modular extensibility

5. Implementation in Skinnova
SHE is implemented as a deterministic prefilter layer over LLM outputs.
5.1 Risk categories
We define four primary semantic risk groups:
(1) Medical terminology
Includes diagnosis, treatment, and therapeutic language.

• weight: 0.4
Represents high regulatory and safety sensitivity.

(2) Absolute claims
Overconfident linguistic constructs such as: always, guaranteed, 100% effective

• weight: 0.2
These correlate strongly with hallucinated certainty.

(3) Brand references
Commercial entities influencing perceived authority or trust.

• weight: 0.3

(4) High-risk ingredient references
Includes pharmacologically active or prescription-strength compounds.

• weight: 0.4

6. Context-aware risk amplification
A key extension is context-dependent risk modulation, particularly for routine generation.
This captures a known failure mode:
generating personalized routines without sufficient user data.

7. Decision mechanism (SHE gate)
Final escalation logic:
if R(x) ≥ 0.25 → send to Judge LLM
else → bypass evaluationThis threshold is empirically tuned to balance:

• safety coverage

• evaluation cost

• system latency

8. Cost model
This results in a substantial reduction in marginal evaluation cost.

9. Design rationale and trade-offs
Advantages:

• deterministic execution

• explainable risk decomposition

• low computational overhead

• scalable across domains

• controllable Judge LLM invocation

Limitations:

• relies on lexical trigger coverage

• limited semantic generalization

• requires manual curation of risk categories

• may miss implicit or paraphrased hallucinations
These are acceptable because SHE is explicitly a pre-filtering mechanism, not a full hallucination detector.

10. Production scalability
SHE is designed as a modular and extensible system.
10.1 Dynamic weight tuning
Weights can be updated using:

• Judge LLM feedback loops

• incident logs

• user escalation patterns

10.2 Adaptive thresholds
Threshold can be adjusted based on:

• domain sensitivity

• regulatory context

• user risk profiles

10.3 Feedback-driven expansion
Judge LLM outputs can refine:

• trigger sets

• category definitions

• false negative reduction

10.4 Multi-tier evaluation architecture
SHE naturally extends into a hierarchical pipeline:
Schema Validation
→ SHE (deterministic gating)
→ Judge LLM
→ Human review (edge cases)Each layer increases:

• cost

• semantic fidelity

• decision authority

11. Conclusion
Selective Hallucination Evaluation reframes hallucination handling as a resource allocation problem over semantic risk space, rather than a uniform correctness evaluation task.
Instead of evaluating every output, SHE enables systems to:
allocate expensive semantic verification only where the probability and impact of hallucination justify it.
In Skinnova, this resulted in:

• significantly reduced Judge LLM usage

• improved latency characteristics

• maintainable safety logic

• scalable evaluation architecture
More broadly, SHE represents a shift from:
“evaluate everything equally”
to:
“evaluate proportionally to risk.”
